message.content: Adversaries may compromise the software supply chain to manipulate application software before it reaches the end consumer, aiming to compromise data or systems. This can involve altering source code, tampering with update mechanisms, or replacing compiled releases with malicious versions. Such attacks can be broad or targeted, moving to further tactics on specific victims. Notable incidents include APT29's trojanized SolarWinds Orion update, APT41's injection of malicious code into signed files, and CCBkdr's inclusion in CCleaner. Other examples include compromised web browser updates by Cobalt Group, trojanized control system software by Dragonfly, and ransomware distribution by GOLD SOUTHFIELD. Mitigation strategies include rigorous code review, secure update mechanisms, integrity checks, and monitoring for unusual activity in software distribution channels. Employing multi-factor authentication, network segmentation, and regular security audits can also help reduce the risk of supply chain compromises.
